sentences.text: I mean, before the ghetto [INAUDIBLE] we have a lot of [INAUDIBLE] and used to kill and grab people and kill.
One Saturday morning, I was walking with my girlfriend, and they grabbed me to work.
And they took me to-- it happened I was lucky-- I went working for a doctor, a German doctor.
When he saw these two girls, he felt so sorry.
He took us upstairs to his office.
He fed us.
He said, don't do nothing.
Just sit.
And then he let us go home.
Sometime, you know, it's up to [? individually. ?] Then later, I used to walk in the street.
The soldiers used to run after us.
Used to be afraid to go out.
I don't know.
I was young.
Not only for me, for young girls and--
I look Jewish, I guess, with my nose.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, long hair.
Yeah.
Yeah, so they brought me my brother from the bunker.
He was already-- after the sickness, which--
That was 1942? '43?
He was 13, so he was already 13 years old.
When the war broke up, he was only nine, 10 years.
And he was already with us together.
So we were laying in that attic then, that labor camp.
And once-- nobody came, nobody brought us anything to eat, because they were afraid to walk in and walk out.
And one guy used to go middle of the night, maybe gonna get something to eat.
So he used to bring a loaf of bread.
But they start fighting, everybody, for a piece of bread.
So I was laying hungry and hungry.
And he had a mother and sister, so he used to, you know--
Give them--
--give them the food.
So finally, the same guy from the estate, [? well, ?] he kept us.
He said, he's got some Polish families.
They gonna take us.
He's gonna come wagons with horses.
And they're going to bring us to the countryside.
Yeah, he was the Gentile.
The Volksdeutsche, they used to say.
He used to help Germans, but in the end, somehow he felt--
So they came.
They took us in the evening.
So they put me to one Gentile Polish family on a farm.
His name was Besolofsky.
He had two sons and a wife.
So they had a bunker under the ground for us.
Was small, maybe four by five.
Four people together.
So I was with three more people, and one lady pregnant.
You didn't have [? the ?] little air there.
You're laying down the ground with no-- we want to play cards here, you couldn't put the candle, nothing.
Wouldn't burn.
Was really tough.
No, not oxygen.
And at the evening, he used to bring us to their little place, where he had on the farm.
And they used to fed us potatoes and sour pickles.
This was our dinner.
At night, back.
One night, I begged the guy.
He had two sons.
My brother was in different place.
He was still with a family, the name was Kotofsky.
He kept maybe 28 people on the top of the barn.
You know.
And I knew my brother's there.
So I begged him if he could take me one night to see my brother.
You have to go just through the woods.
So he say, OK.
For me, his son will take me.
So one evening, he took me through the woods, and I went to see my brother.
He was fine with another 28 people, all laying in the hay, in the top of the stable, you know.
And this was it.
Later, that guy where I was hiding, Besolofsky, he got scared.
Somebody told, he knows he keeps Jews.
He say, you have to all go.
So that poor pregnant woman has to have a baby.
So we all left different place.
I went to the same place where my brother was still there.
That woman, I think she came to-- we all went there, for the beginning.
We were there for couple weeks.
They took all of you?
Yeah.
He kept everybody.
He said, if I'm gonna live through the war, and I'm gonna keep the Jews, I'm going to be someday in history.
He was a very good nature.
So we came there.
We stayed for a while.
There was like, at Christmas Eve.
He killed two pigs to feed us.
He used to bring us some meat and breakfast, you know?
He gave us more to eat.
And it was really, we couldn't complain.
We couldn't get something better.
You should perhaps spell his name, because he did such a-- he should be remembered.
Kotofsky-- But he's not alive, they killed him.
You know.
Kotofsky.
One evening-- it  was Christmas Eve-- we hear shots.
He was doing his work outside with his wife, and the family was inside.
So it was not the Germans.
It was the Ukrainian came.
And they shot the whole family, him and his wife outside, and they burned the whole houses.
And then we're laying upstairs, on the top of the stables.
And I hear how one say to another, now we gonna burn the-- in Polish, they call [POLISH], stables, whatever.
When we heard this, we say, we gonna get burned, let's go and start running.
Snow was up to here.
Every footprint you could see.
And we start all running, and they're after us with the machine guns.
And not one got killed.
We all got safe.
The footprint could bring you, even after, so we start running through the woods.
My girlfriend, she fell to a lake.
She froze her feet.
She had no shoes, anyways.
We all was cold.
And she couldn't walk anymore.
And I have to take on my shoulder.
We used to-- my brother, and then other people help her to walk.
And we came to the woods, what are we doing now?
Finally, we found some Polish family again.
And we walked in.
They took us in.
They separate us.
Some people-- that pregnant woman, with her-- he was not her husband even, I don't think so-- left back to Brody, where we came from.
She was-- she delivered the baby.
He was shot, and she was killed.
And the baby's still there till today.
Nobody knows who has the baby.
And a few more people left.
So we were like a group, one, two three, four, about five, six, about 10, 12 people left.
And we got to a family--
Yeah, some of them went to different place.
Then [INAUDIBLE] we used to meet different people in the woods.
And we came the one Polish family.
And if we have any money, they would take us, keep us.
And da da da, OK.
And other guy had, he pay.
And this one took off her jacket, this one take off-- everything was good for them.
They had nothing.
So he said to me one day and to my friend-- she couldn't walk, her feet was frozen-- you stay here.
And my brother was young, you.
There was another guy, he said, you stay here.
Those people-- no, he took them day before to another place they're gonna keep them.
And you people, the rest, he say, next day, you're gonna walk through the woods [? during the day. ?] He told, one guy was-- he came from there.
He was living not far from the little country before the war.
He say he knows how to get there.
We going in the woods to meet the rest of our people.
We walking.
And the first thing, I saw somebody behind the tree with a gun and start shooting at us.
And I start screaming, let's run.
Somebody's here and shooting.
I'm only one survivor.
The rest all died.
But stupid watch, they took off.
They took off the jackets from the people.
That's why they kill the people.
I was running--
The Polacks, the Ukraine, who the hell knows.
From that group, they wanted to give us the places to stay.
They were going for the jewelry, for the things.
I was only one.
That was miracles.
I don't know.
Maybe they did special, they didn't want to shot me.
They knew my brother is there, my friend.
Or it just happened.
I was running like the wind was pushing me, you know?
Terrible.
I say, now, what I'm going to do in the woods?
I don't know where I've been in woods.
You know, I could get lost.
Is it still snowing?
All along, snowing.
Cold, no clothes.
What I'm going to do all alone?
If I had somebody at least, and know where to go, what to do.
And I'm running.
And I see two guys.
And I was afraid that another Polacks they're gonna kill me, or Ukraine.
I start running different direction.
He stopped, he say, don't run.
We are Jews.
We gonna help you.
What happened, the two boys were waiting his sister with the baby was with us, and her husband.
And they got killed.
You understand?
So I said, I don't know what to do now.
He said, my sister was with you, with her husband and the baby.
I said, they all got killed.
So he say, don't worry.
We take you.
I know where you supposed to go.
He was waiting for us too, he wants to meet his sister.
He took me back to where my brother was.
And my girlfriend was there, and other guy.
We were going from place to place till finally one family kept us for a while.
And one day, they come and say, the German know the Jews are hiding here in the woods, and by us, and someplace else.
They gonna come and kill us all.
And we thought, it's just, you know.
Next day-- morning, they came.
They burned the whole, the little countryside.
The people, the little houses, everybody got killed.
We all start running to the woods.
We go back in the woods.
And my brother in different direction, so I lost him completely.
Being in the woods.
No place to hide.
It's snow.
The trees, no leaves.
Nothing.
If somebody walks in, right away, could see you.
I'm staying like this on the side, and I'm looking.
I see two Gestapo with white capes, with machine guns, going in the woods and looking like this.
I said to my friend-- another boy came, I don't know  from where he came-- I said, let's hide here.
He say, where you going to hide?
There's no trees, no bushes.
Everything is so empty.
Somehow, we made to the under the bush.
And they're walking and looking, started to look here, to look to the other side, and they missed us.
What about your footprints?
They were looking, but somehow they missed it.
You know how they say, miracles.
So we stay all day in the woods.
In the evening, we walked out.
Everything, everything was burnt.
Was no place where to go.
No place what to do.
No place where to get food.
So we want to the woods.
We were laying in the woods.
We put fire.
I grab a blanket from somebody, I don't know where.
I used to fall asleep, tired.
The blanket caught fire, and I didn't have the blanket, even.
And everyday, we used to run from one place to another.
How long was this last--?
Well, this was till '44.
Almost.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we start living in the woods, making fire.
And finally then they burned the whole country, so we found cows dead, horses.
We used to go out, cut meat and make steaks on the fire.
[LAUGHS] We have to eat something.
We found shoes from people we need.
We found clothing.
Some people were dead.
No, you have to.
We used to step on dead people.
Polish, who knows, maybe but he was Jews, hiding something, you never know.
So we were sitting, sitting in the wood day after day.
No, it was more people.
We got together, used to find people in the woods.
Everybody was wandering.
And we were--
I didn't have summer.
We left the [INAUDIBLE] summer during-- the ghetto, we start, like, '42 in December.
Then the liquidation was '43 May.
So I had-- that labor camp, we were in the summer.
Then through the winter, we were already in the hidings.
Yeah.
Then we were hidings for weeks, for months.
Every day, sometime you sit in the woods.
A dog shows up, you think somebody's smelling-- you know, dog comes, maybe the Germans are.
We start walking to a different place.
[INAUDIBLE] one day, we were wandering, and we found a empty little house.
And we walked in.
We found potatoes and a big oven.
My brother was cold.
And we start making fire.
And baked--
Somehow, we found matches, something.
[INAUDIBLE] Because we used to start fire in the-- Some people used to go during the night to some homes far away and ask for bread, ask for this, you know?
You have to.
Risk.
So we start fire, and we start baking potatoes.
And my brother went on the top, that-- you didn't see the old-fashioned ovens-- in the top, and fell asleep.
And I forgot completely about it.
And we were so lucky.
It's a little warm, we bake potatoes, gonna eat.
And all of a sudden, I look out the window.
All the houses with Ukraine with shotguns.
I have to schlep my brother on my shoulder sleeping.
And we start running.
The snow's up to here again.
We had lot of snows there.
And they're after us with the shotgun.
I'm falling down and get up.
I'm falling down and get up.
And they shot-- and they couldn't.
Nobody got hurt.
We went to the woods, was not far in the woods.
And they let us alone.
They didn't go through the wood after us.
So we were, again, in the woods with no house.
And that's how we were living in the woods for the months and months and months.
One evening, we walked in the woods, I found a little things to store potatoes for the winter.
So we used to go down at night to sleep in that little-- you know, little hole, was nothing to it.
It was much warmer than today in the snow.
And grab potatoes next morning, go out to the fire, bake potatoes.
Go out, find a cow someplace laying, and make steaks.
Horseradish, we found a lot.
We used to bake horseradish-- everything was good.
I don't know, we were lucky, and nobody got sick, ever.
You know.
Nobody got too sick, nobody got cold.
I didn't had.
I got the little skirt with the little sweater on me.
That's how I left the house, that's how I was.
Well, we used to, from people sometime, that people used to grab something.
Otherwise, I had no clothes.
I can't think today how I could live without.
We used to go and go with to that little bunker for potatoes.
And one night, we walk there, we sleep.
And next morning, I wake up.
We want to go out, and I see horses, wagons, soldiers.
So I was sure that the German.
So we start running back to hide.
And they start with the little flags to us.
Don't run, we are Russians.
Don't run.
So we came back.
They say the partisan Russians.
And that not far from here, they're liberated already.
Russian are close.
I don't remember the date.
It was like March in '44.
And they took us close to their wagons.
They give us something to eat.
They give us blankets.
And they say, know what, in the evening, we gonna you to the Russian side.
OK.
So we spent the day there with them, and they fed us.
And we were warm already, clothes.
And they take us in the evening with the wagons to a little town.
I don't remember even the name.
And we staying overnight in one empty house they gave us.
We wake up next morning.
The German came back.
The Russian disappear, and we are stuck on the Germans again.
So what we do in case like this?
Daylight.
Start running back, walking through the woods.
And we walked again through the woods.
We were sometime again in the woods.
And then we say we have to try to get out, maybe the Russian are back already.
They were so close.
It was one guy who was living in not far from a little town they called [?
Batkami ?].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And say, let's walk to [?
Batkami ?].
I know the way how to get there.
And we going, and we going.
Finally, we found already the little town [?
Batkami ?].
So the Russian were there.
So we walk again [INAUDIBLE] what they used to call the office from the Russian.
And we walking in, and they look at us.
They going to arrest us.
They say we are spies.
So we say, we are Jews.
We're not spy.
He say, you are spy.
And they want to arrest us.
So the guy who used to live before the war in that little town, he said, I know people here.
Maybe they gonna recognize me.
We are not spy, please leave us alone.
We went through so much.
So they called a Polack.
He came, he recognize us, so they let us free.
They let us free.
So we start walking.
You walk always.
You don't have a car, you don't have horses.
You walk to-- from town to town.
This one give us something to eat, this one give us something to wear.
Finally, we came to one little town, [?
Shimenitz ?], they call.
They were already Russian, a couple Jews I met.
And we were staying there for awhile.
After that, went to one town, the name Dubno.
There, I figure, we gonna already stay for good.
Because where I came from, Brody, they were still fighting.
Took another six months till the Brody was liberated.
So we stay in Dubno.
And we took a little tiny house, two rooms.
Me and my girlfriend and my brother.
And we staying in that house.
And what happen?
I'm getting sick.
I'm getting typhus.
I was lucky I didn't got in the woods, otherwise I wouldn't be here today.
So they took me to the-- my girlfriend was a nurse in the hospital working.
They took me to the hospital.
I was laying, and a high temperature fever I had.
And I had long hair.
They want to cut my hair, and I cried, don't you touch my hair.
So they never cut, just short-- Why did they want to cut it?
Did it have lice?
If you have typhus, later you lose hair.
So if you cut them, they grow nicer later.
And I didn't let them cut, just shorter.
And I came out, I was skinny and really sick-looking.
I said, what I'm gonna do now?
So I'm trying to go back to my town, finally was liberated.
My girlfriend, she was a nurse in that hospital.
We save her life, me and my brother.
She had no money.
She fell in the lake.
She couldn't go out for food.
My brother used to bring.
And she say, if I live through, you gonna have everything.
Because she knew where her family left all her money, jewelry, [INAUDIBLE], everything.
I said, fine, look.
So we stay in Dubno.
When they were liberated, I just came out from the hospital.
I couldn't go to my town, Brody.
She went alone.
And I stay in Dubno.
And she came back.
She didn't help me much.
She became new friends, she got friendly with somebody, and she gave-- which was OK.
My brother, we met some people, Russia.
They used to bring all kind of stuff.
He used to go in those flea markets, sell it.
He make, here and there, couple rubles, they used to call, just to make a living.
We were staying.
One day, I say, I'm going to take a ride to Brody.
Maybe we gonna settle there.
We going to find a little house, and we going to stay there.
We gonna see what's going to happen.
And I'm going there.
And I'm sitting on the truck.
We used to grab, you know, American trucks, they used to take us.
And I'm driving that street where my aunt used to work.
And that woman used to be there like a super there, take care, cleaning, this.
And I see she wears my sister clothes.
And I got so sick, I couldn't look at her.
But still, I went to my friend to visit.
And everybody, why don't you take his house, their house.
Settle here.
Yeah, it was empty.
All the Jewish homes.
And people after the war, they-- they were running, they didn't know where they're going.
What to Germany, [INAUDIBLE].
I say no, I can't live here.
I can't, somehow, you know?
And I went back to Dubno.
Went back to Dubno.
Somehow I used to take a little merchandise, bring to another town, and-- Was this in 19-- what?
In 1944 already.
Well, where we were was sort of liberated.
My town, and where I was staying, there was still fighting.
But-- then I met my husband.
And I married.
Yeah.
He moved before I married.
He wants to marry.
I didn't want to get married, so he moved to Lwow, Lemberg, big city.
He was already in business.
He was--
He was a little older than me.
And after couple days, I say, he left, he wants to marry me.
I should marry, not marry.
Then I say to myself, I'm gonna take a ride to Lwow.
So my girlfriend's husband say, you know what, I go with you.
Because she was afraid to leave me alone on a train at night, you know, the Russian, and-- We went on the train all night till we stop in one place.
In Lwow, we took a room [INAUDIBLE].
Next day, I went to visit him.
So he say he settled there, he's in business already.
He's working and--
Black market.
Gold, [INAUDIBLE], dollars and everything.
As always.
And I decide to marry and stay there.
And my brother came back to stay with me.
When he came back, he didn't like it.
So he went to Israel.
No, first we went to Germany, yeah.
We live in Lwow for some time.
Then we were thinking to go to Germany.
From Germany, we will be able go to United States [? best. ?]
We left like September.
We came to Germany 1946.
I remember.
Because before--
Yeah, Lwow.
Then we went to Przemysl.
Then we went to Krakow, we stay.
Then we went to German-- first to Austria.
We stay in Linz for some time in a DP camp.
Then finally, we went to Germany.
We stayed in DP camp.
Pocking.
Deggendorf.
In Germany already.
Deggendorf.
Hof.
And Lechfeld by Augsburg.
DP camps.
Well, the first camp, Pocking, we used to live in little barracks.
One tiny room.
Was really hard.
Then they liquidate sometime, that sent the people away.
So we went to another camp, which we had much better apartments.
Then I came to Lechfeld.
We had a villa, which the SS men used to live there.
And I had a baby already.
You know.
